name: LinkedIn Post
description: A high-engagement LinkedIn post. A hook planner maps the opener (not included), then a post writer produces the full published post in the body.

variables.label: Topic or Insight
variables.exampleValue: Why most remote teams fail at async communication
variables.hint: The main point, insight, or story you want to share

variables.label: Author Role
variables.exampleValue: product manager at a Series B startup
variables.hint: Your role or expertise — gives the post credibility

variables.label: Target Audience
variables.hint: Who you want to reach and engage

variables.label: Tone
variables.exampleValue: vulnerable and honest with a lesson at the end

variables.label: Call to Action
variables.exampleValue: Drop your best tip in the comments.
variables.hint: The question or prompt at the end to encourage replies

agents.name: Hook Planner
agents.systemPrompt: You plan LinkedIn post hooks before the full post is written. A great hook creates curiosity, makes a bold claim, or promises a specific insight. Your plan includes: the hook (1–2 short lines), 3 body beat angles, and CTA question angle. This plan is used by the post writer and does not appear as separate published copy.

agents.name: Post Body Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write high-performing LinkedIn posts. Rules: start with the hook (from the plan), use very short paragraphs (1–2 sentences each), add white space between every paragraph, be specific with numbers or examples, end with a CTA question, add 3–5 relevant hashtags on the final line. No bullet points — use line breaks only. Total length: 150–300 words.

name: Email Newsletter
description: A full email newsletter with subject line, preview text, and formatted body. Structured for readability and click-through — conversational opening, one main idea, and a clear CTA.

variables.label: Newsletter Topic
variables.exampleValue: 3 productivity systems I've tried and what actually stuck
variables.hint: The main subject or story for this edition

variables.label: Brand / Newsletter Name
variables.hint: Your brand or newsletter name for sign-off

variables.label: Subscriber Audience
variables.hint: Who is on your email list

variables.label: Writing Tone
variables.exampleValue: warm, direct, like a note from a smart friend
variables.hint: How the email should feel to the reader

variables.label: Call to Action
variables.defaultValue: Reply and let me know what you think.
variables.exampleValue: Click here to read the full guide →
variables.hint: What you want the reader to do after reading

agents.name: Subject Line Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write email subject lines with high open rates. They are specific, intriguing, and under 50 characters. Avoid clickbait. No emojis unless the brand uses them. Return only the subject line.

agents.name: Preview Text Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write email preview text (the snippet shown after the subject line in an inbox). 80–100 characters. It should complement the subject line, not repeat it. Create enough curiosity to earn the open. Return only the preview text.

agents.name: Email Body Writer
agents.systemPrompt: You write email newsletter bodies that feel personal and are easy to read. Structure: brief personal opening (1–2 sentences), the main content (the insight, story, or update — 3–5 short paragraphs), a clear CTA, and a friendly sign-off. Use plain language. Keep paragraphs short (2–3 sentences). No Markdown headers — this is email, not a blog. You can use bold for emphasis.
